subject: Economics
chapter: Simple Applications of Tools of Demand and Supply

definitions.definition: The law of demand states that, ceteris paribus, as the price of a commodity increases, the quantity demanded of it decreases, and vice versa.
definitions.example: When the price of a bag of potato chips increases, the quantity demanded decreases.

definitions.definition: A graphical representation of the relationship between the price of a commodity and the quantity demanded of it, ceteris paribus.
definitions.example: A demand curve shows how the quantity demanded of a commodity changes as its price changes.

definitions.definition: A graphical representation of the relationship between the price of a commodity and the quantity supplied of it, ceteris paribus.
definitions.example: A supply curve shows how the quantity supplied of a commodity changes as its price changes.

definitions.definition: The law of supply states that, ceteris paribus, as the price of a commodity increases, the quantity supplied of it increases, and vice versa.
definitions.example: When the price of a commodity increases, the quantity supplied also increases.

definitions.definition: A Latin phrase meaning 'all other things being equal,' used to assume that all other factors remain constant when analyzing the relationship between two variables.
definitions.example: Ceteris paribus implies that other factors like income, tastes, and technology remain constant.

definitions.definition: A period of time in which some factors of production, such as capital, remain fixed, while others, like labor, can be adjusted.
definitions.example: In the short-run, a firm can adjust its labor input but not its capital input.

definitions.definition: A period of time in which all factors of production, including capital, can be adjusted.
definitions.example: In the long-run, a firm can adjust both its labor and capital inputs.

definitions.definition: The price at which the quantity demanded of a commodity equals the quantity supplied, resulting in no excess demand or supply.
definitions.example: When the market price equals the equilibrium price, the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied.

definitions.definition: The quantity of a commodity that is bought and sold at the equilibrium price, resulting in no excess demand or supply.
definitions.example: When the market quantity equals the equilibrium quantity, the market is in equilibrium.

definitions.definition: A situation in which the quantity demanded of a commodity equals the quantity supplied, resulting in no excess demand or supply.
definitions.example: Market equilibrium occurs when the supply curve intersects the demand curve.

definitions.definition: A situation in which the quantity supplied of a commodity exceeds the quantity demanded, resulting in excess supply.
definitions.example: A surplus occurs when the supply curve lies above the demand curve.

definitions.definition: A situation in which the quantity demanded of a commodity exceeds the quantity supplied, resulting in excess demand.
definitions.example: A shortage occurs when the demand curve lies above the supply curve.

definitions.definition: A situation in which a consumer maximizes their satisfaction or utility from a given budget constraint.
definitions.example: Consumer equilibrium occurs when the marginal utility of a good equals its price.

definitions.definition: A situation in which a producer maximizes their profits from a given production constraint.
definitions.example: Producer equilibrium occurs when the marginal revenue of a good equals its marginal cost.

definitions.definition: A graphical representation of the combinations of two goods that provide a consumer with the same level of satisfaction or utility.
definitions.example: An indifference curve shows how a consumer's preferences change as the price of a good changes.

definitions.definition: A graphical representation of the combinations of two goods that a consumer can afford to buy given their budget constraint.
definitions.example: A budget line shows how a consumer's spending power changes as the price of a good changes.
